was the championship series in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") for the [1948 season](/wiki/1948_Major_League_Baseball_season "1948 Major League Baseball season"). The 45th edition of the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), it matched the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) champion [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/1948_Cleveland_Indians_season "1948 Cleveland Indians season") and the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) champion [Boston Braves](/wiki/1948_Boston_Braves_season "1948 Boston Braves season"). The Braves had won the National League pennant for the first time since the "Miracle Braves" team of [1914](/wiki/1914_World_Series "1914 World Series"), while the Indians had spoiled a chance for the only all-Boston World Series by winning a [one-game playoff](/wiki/1948_American_League_tie-breaker_game "1948 American League tie-breaker game") against the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1948_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1948 Boston Red Sox season") for the American League flag.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Though superstar [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") failed to win either of his two starts, the Indians won the Series in six games to capture their second championship (as well as their most recent) and their first since [1920](/wiki/1920_World_Series "1920 World Series").

The **1948 World Series** was the championship series in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") for the [1948 season](/wiki/1948_Major_League_Baseball_season "1948 Major League Baseball season"). The 45th edition of the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series"), it matched the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League") (AL) champion [Cleveland Indians](/wiki/1948_Cleveland_Indians_season "1948 Cleveland Indians season") and the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(baseball) "National League (baseball)") (NL) champion [Boston Braves](/wiki/1948_Boston_Braves_season "1948 Boston Braves season"). The Braves had won the National League pennant for the first time since the "Miracle Braves" team of [1914](/wiki/1914_World_Series "1914 World Series"), while the Indians had spoiled a chance for the only all-Boston World Series by winning a [one-game playoff](/wiki/1948_American_League_tie-breaker_game "1948 American League tie-breaker game") against the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/1948_Boston_Red_Sox_season "1948 Boston Red Sox season") for the American League flag.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Though superstar [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") failed to win either of his two starts, the Indians won the Series in six games to capture their second championship (as well as their most recent) and their first since [1920](/wiki/1920_World_Series "1920 World Series").

It was the first World Series to be televised beyond the previous year's limited New York-Schenectady-Philadelphia-Baltimore-Washington network and was announced by famed sportcasters [Red Barber](/wiki/Red_Barber "Red Barber"), [Tom Hussey](/wiki/Tom_Hussey "Tom Hussey") (in Boston) and [Van Patrick](/wiki/Van_Patrick "Van Patrick") (in Cleveland).[[2]](#cite_note-2) This was the second appearance in the Fall Classic for both teams, with the Indians' lone previous appearance coming in a 1920 win against the [Brooklyn Dodgers](/wiki/History_of_the_Brooklyn_Dodgers "History of the Brooklyn Dodgers") and the Braves' lone previous appearance coming in a 1914 win against the [Philadelphia Athletics](/wiki/History_of_the_Philadelphia_Athletics "History of the Philadelphia Athletics"). Consequently, this was the first, and to date only, World Series in which both participating teams had previously played in, but not yet lost, a previous World Series. Currently, this phenomenon can only be repeated if the [Miami Marlins](/wiki/Miami_Marlins "Miami Marlins") or the [Washington Nationals](/wiki/Washington_Nationals "Washington Nationals") play against the [Los Angeles Angels](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Angels "Los Angeles Angels") in a future World Series.

Television coverage of the World Series increased this year, but due to the medium still being in its infancy coverage was strictly regional. The series was open to any channel with an affiliation with one of the national broadcast networks: [NBC](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_NBC "Major League Baseball on NBC"), [CBS](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_CBS "Major League Baseball on CBS"), [ABC](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_ABC "Major League Baseball on ABC"), or [DuMont](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball_on_DuMont "Major League Baseball on DuMont"). But games played in Boston could only be seen in the Northeast, while when the series shifted to Cleveland those games were the first to be aired in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh"), [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), [St. Louis](/wiki/St._Louis "St. Louis"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit") and [Toledo](/wiki/Toledo,_Ohio "Toledo, Ohio").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

This was the only World Series played between [1947](/wiki/1947_World_Series "1947 World Series") and [1958](/wiki/1958_World_Series "1958 World Series") not to feature a [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") team, and the last not won by a New York team until the [1957 Series](/wiki/1957_World_Series "1957 World Series") (which the Braves, having relocated to [Milwaukee](/wiki/Milwaukee "Milwaukee"), won over the Yankees). The two teams would meet again in the [1995 Series](/wiki/1995_World_Series "1995 World Series"), with the Braves—by then relocated to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta")—winning. This was the first World Series, and the last until [2016](/wiki/2016_World_Series "2016 World Series"), in which both teams scored the same number of total runs. This would be the final World Series to feature a team helmed by a player-manager. This is also the only World Series pre-1967 in which the winning team had not won at least one [Commissioner's Trophy](/wiki/Commissioner%27s_Trophy_(MLB) "Commissioner's Trophy (MLB)") after its introduction in the [1967 World Series](/wiki/1967_World_Series "1967 World Series").

As of 2026, this is Cleveland’s last World Series victory, and they now hold the longest championship drought in the majors.

| **[WP](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record_(pitching)#Winning_pitcher "Win–loss record (pitching)"):** [Johnny Sain](/wiki/Johnny_Sain "Johnny Sain") (1–0)   **[LP](/wiki/Win%E2%80%93loss_record_(pitching)#Losing_pitcher "Win–loss record (pitching)"):** [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") (0–1) | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | | |

Braves pitcher [Johnny Sain](/wiki/Johnny_Sain "Johnny Sain") and Indians pitcher [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") were engaged in a scoreless pitchers' duel when the Braves came to bat in the bottom of the eighth [inning](/wiki/Inning "Inning"). Feller [walked](/wiki/Base_on_balls "Base on balls") Braves catcher [Bill Salkeld](/wiki/Bill_Salkeld "Bill Salkeld") to open the inning. Braves manager, [Billy Southworth](/wiki/Billy_Southworth "Billy Southworth") then replaced the slow-footed Salkeld with [Phil Masi](/wiki/Phil_Masi "Phil Masi"), who entered the game as a [pinch runner](/wiki/Pinch_runner "Pinch runner"). [Mike McCormick](/wiki/Mike_McCormick_(outfielder) "Mike McCormick (outfielder)") followed with a [sacrifice bunt](/wiki/Sacrifice_bunt "Sacrifice bunt"), advancing Masi to [second base](/wiki/Second_base "Second base"). Feller issued an [intentional walk](/wiki/Intentional_walk "Intentional walk") to [Eddie Stanky](/wiki/Eddie_Stanky "Eddie Stanky"), who was replaced by [Sibby Sisti](/wiki/Sibby_Sisti "Sibby Sisti"). Feller then tried to [pick off](/wiki/Pick_off "Pick off") Masi at second base. Indians' shortstop [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau") appeared to tag Masi [out](/wiki/Out_(baseball) "Out (baseball)"), but [umpire](/wiki/Umpire_(baseball) "Umpire (baseball)") Bill Stewart called him [safe](/wiki/Safe_(baseball) "Safe (baseball)").[[11]](#cite_note-storm-11) [Tommy Holmes](/wiki/Tommy_Holmes "Tommy Holmes") proceeded to hit a [single](/wiki/Single_(baseball) "Single (baseball)") that allowed Masi to score the only [run](/wiki/Run_(baseball) "Run (baseball)") of the game, giving the Braves a 1–0 victory.[[5]](#cite_note-Game1-5)

The umpire's controversial ruling touched off heated debates among the media and fans,[[11]](#cite_note-storm-11) especially after [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") photographs of the play were published.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Although Feller allowed only two hits, he took the loss in what would be the closest he came to winning a World Series game.[[11]](#cite_note-storm-11) Upon his death in 1990, Masi's will revealed that he really was out on the pick-off play.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

The second game also made television history when a live broadcast of the Indians–Braves matchup was shown aboard the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad")'s [*Marylander*](/wiki/Marylander_(train) "Marylander (train)") passenger train travelling between Washington, D.C., and New York City, using a receiver operated by [Bendix Corporation](/wiki/Bendix_Corporation "Bendix Corporation") technicians.[[15]](#cite_note-NYT48-15) An [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") reporter observing the demonstration said, "Technically, it was surprisingly good."[[15]](#cite_note-NYT48-15) The Braves scored a run in the first off [Bob Lemon](/wiki/Bob_Lemon "Bob Lemon") on [Bob Elliott](/wiki/Bob_Elliott_(baseball) "Bob Elliott (baseball)")'s RBI single with two on, but Lemon held them scoreless for the rest of the game. After three shutout innings, [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau") hit a leadoff double in the fourth off [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn"), then scored on [Joe Gordon](/wiki/Joe_Gordon "Joe Gordon")'s single with Gordon advancing to second on the throw to home. One out later, [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby")'s RBI single put the Indians up 2–1. Next inning, [Dale Mitchell](/wiki/Dale_Mitchell_(baseball) "Dale Mitchell (baseball)") hit a leadoff single, moved to second on a sacrifice bunt and scored on Boudreau's single. The Indians scored one more run in the ninth off [Nels Potter](/wiki/Nels_Potter "Nels Potter") when [Jim Hegan](/wiki/Jim_Hegan "Jim Hegan") reached on an error, moved to third on two groundouts and scored on [Bob Kennedy](/wiki/Bob_Kennedy "Bob Kennedy")'s single. The series was tied 1–1 heading to Cleveland.

For the third straight game, no home runs were hit by either team. This would not happen again in a World Series until [2014](/wiki/2014_World_Series "2014 World Series"). The game's two runs came on [Larry Doby](/wiki/Larry_Doby "Larry Doby")'s groundout in the third after a double and walk and [Jim Hegan](/wiki/Jim_Hegan "Jim Hegan")'s RBI single after a single and walk in the fourth, both off [Vern Bickford](/wiki/Vern_Bickford "Vern Bickford"). [Gene Bearden](/wiki/Gene_Bearden "Gene Bearden") pitched a complete shutout, allowing five hits while striking out four, as the Indians took a 2–1 series lead.

[Satchel Paige](/wiki/Satchel_Paige "Satchel Paige") appeared for the Indians, becoming the first black pitcher to take the mound in World Series history. The previous day's single-game attendance record was broken with 86,288 fans. After two leadoff singles, [Bob Elliott](/wiki/Bob_Elliott_(baseball) "Bob Elliott (baseball)")'s three-run home run in the first off Indians starter [Bob Feller](/wiki/Bob_Feller "Bob Feller") made it 3–0 Braves. [Dale Mitchell](/wiki/Dale_Mitchell_(baseball) "Dale Mitchell (baseball)")'s leadoff home run in the bottom half off [Nels Potter](/wiki/Nels_Potter "Nels Potter") put the Indians on the board. Elliott's second home run of the game in the third made it 4–1 Braves, but in the fourth after a leadoff single and walk, [Walt Judnich](/wiki/Walt_Judnich "Walt Judnich")'s RBI single made it 4–2 Braves, then one out later, [Jim Hegan](/wiki/Jim_Hegan "Jim Hegan")'s three-run home run put the Indians in front 5–4 and knock Potter out of the game. [Bill Salkeld](/wiki/Bill_Salkeld "Bill Salkeld")'s home run in the sixth tied the game. Next inning, [Tommy Holmes](/wiki/Tommy_Holmes "Tommy Holmes") hit a leadoff single, moved to second on a sacrifice bunt, and scored on [Earl Torgeson](/wiki/Earl_Torgeson "Earl Torgeson")'s RBI single. [Ed Klieman](/wiki/Ed_Klieman "Ed Klieman") relieved Feller and allowed a walk, two-runs single to [Marv Rickert](/wiki/Marv_Rickert "Marv Rickert"), and another walk. [Russ Christopher](/wiki/Russ_Christopher "Russ Christopher") then allowed RBI singles to [Mike McCormick](/wiki/Mike_McCormick_(outfielder) "Mike McCormick (outfielder)") and [Eddie Stanky](/wiki/Eddie_Stanky "Eddie Stanky"). [Warren Spahn](/wiki/Warren_Spahn "Warren Spahn")'s sacrifice fly off Paige capped the game's scoring at 11–5. Spahn pitched 5+2⁄3 shutout innings of relief for the win, forcing a Game 6 in Boston.

The Indians struck first in Game 6 when [Dale Mitchell](/wiki/Dale_Mitchell_(baseball) "Dale Mitchell (baseball)") hit a leadoff double in the third off [Bill Voiselle](/wiki/Bill_Voiselle "Bill Voiselle") and scored on [Lou Boudreau](/wiki/Lou_Boudreau "Lou Boudreau")'s RBI double, but the Braves tied the game on [Mike McCormick](/wiki/Mike_McCormick_(outfielder) "Mike McCormick (outfielder)")'s RBI single with two on off [Bob Lemon](/wiki/Bob_Lemon "Bob Lemon") in the fourth. A walk loaded the bases, but Voiselle grounded out to end the inning. [Joe Gordon](/wiki/Joe_Gordon "Joe Gordon")'s leadoff home in the sixth put the Indians back in front 2–1. After a one-out walk and single, [Jim Hegan](/wiki/Jim_Hegan "Jim Hegan")'s RBI groundout extended their lead to 3–1. Three straight singles in the eighth by [Ken Keltner](/wiki/Ken_Keltner "Ken Keltner"), [Thurman Tucker](/wiki/Thurman_Tucker "Thurman Tucker") and [Eddie Robinson](/wiki/Eddie_Robinson_(baseball) "Eddie Robinson (baseball)") made it 4–1 Indians. In the bottom of the inning, the Braves loaded the bases off Lemon on a single, double and walk. [Clint Conatser](/wiki/Clint_Conatser "Clint Conatser")'s sacrifice fly and [Phil Masi](/wiki/Phil_Masi "Phil Masi")'s RBI double off [Gene Bearden](/wiki/Gene_Bearden "Gene Bearden") made it 4–3 Indians, but Bearden pitched a scoreless ninth for the save to give the Indians the championship.

To date, this is Cleveland’s last World Series victory, and they now hold the longest championship drought in the majors. The only team of the four major North American leagues with a longer championship drought are the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL")’s [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/Arizona_Cardinals "Arizona Cardinals"), who last won a championship in [1947](/wiki/1947_NFL_Championship_Game "1947 NFL Championship Game") as the [Chicago Cardinals](/wiki/Chicago_Cardinals "Chicago Cardinals"), a year before Cleveland’s World Series victory.
